questions.question: How does Pagefind serve search results on a static Jekyll site?
choices: It runs a small server-side process that queries an external index.
It builds an index at build time and ships JavaScript that searches the index client-side.
It proxies queries to Google Custom Search.
It re-runs Jekyll on every query to find matching posts.
questions.explanation: Pagefind is a build-time tool - it walks `_site/` after `jekyll build`, writes a chunked index plus a UI bundle, and the entire search runs in the reader's browser. No server, no third party seeing queries.

questions.question: You're choosing between giscus and a Mastodon thread embed for comments on your engineering blog. Which trade-off is correctly stated?
choices: giscus requires readers to have a GitHub account; Mastodon embed requires you to have a Mastodon presence to host the discussion.
giscus is paid; Mastodon embed is free.
giscus stores comments in your repo; Mastodon embed needs a database.
giscus only works in dark mode; Mastodon embed only works in light mode.
questions.explanation: giscus uses GitHub Discussions as the backend, so commenters need GitHub accounts (a filter on non-developer readers). The Mastodon embed pattern means you post the link on Mastodon and embed the resulting thread - which only works if you maintain a Mastodon presence. The other options are false.

questions.question: Which of these is the right trigger to start planning a migration off GitHub Pages?
choices: You've seen Reddit threads saying Pages is uncool.
Your build consistently exceeds the 10-minute timeout, or you need server-side capabilities Pages doesn't support.
Your site has more than 50 posts.
You want to add a contact form.
questions.explanation: The honest migration triggers are concrete: bumping a documented limit (build timeout, bandwidth, repo size) or needing server-side functionality. Post count alone doesn't matter. A contact form can be handled by a third-party static form service without leaving Pages. "Pages is uncool" is the vibes-driven version that the chapter warns against.
